title: The oldest tree
summary: A mathematics teacher walks with his daughter in the Singapore Botanical Gardens. She wants the answer. He says he does not know.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Father, this tree is so big.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Yes, Lin. It is very big.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Is this the oldest tree in the garden?

body.speaker: Father
body.text: I do not know.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: You always know. You are a teacher.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: I am a teacher of mathematics. Not of trees.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: The sign says one hundred and eighty years.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Yes. That is old. But it is not the answer.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Why don't you guess?

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Because a guess is not a fact.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: But you guess at home. You guess for the weather. You guess for dinner.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: That is true. But this is a garden. A garden has people who know.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Where?

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Look. The woman in the green hat. She works here. She knows.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Ask her.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: We will ask her. But Lin, listen first. It is good to say 'I do not know.' If I say 'maybe one hundred and eighty,' you will believe me. You will tell your friends. Your friends will tell their friends. Soon a wrong number is everywhere.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: I understand.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Now we go ask. What is the question?

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: 'Madam, is this the oldest tree in the garden?'

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Good. Slow voice. Polite.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Madam, is this the oldest tree?

body.speaker: Woman
body.text: No. The oldest tree is by the lake. Two hundred and twelve years old.

body.speaker: Lin
body.text: Father, you were right to not guess.

body.speaker: Father
body.text: Today you learn two things — about trees, and about questions.

annotations.meaningVi: già nhất / cổ nhất (dạng so sánh nhất của 'old')
con/tôi hiểu rồi (lời xác nhận hiểu ý)
thưa cô / thưa bà (cách gọi lịch sự với phụ nữ)
cạnh cái hồ / ở gần hồ ('by' = ở gần)
sao bạn không ... ? (cách gợi ý hoặc hỏi lý do lịch sự)
đúng khi không đoán ('right to + động từ' = đúng khi làm gì đó)

grammarNotes.title: 'I do not know' and asking polite questions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu phủ định trong tiếng Anh dùng 'do not / does not + động từ nguyên thể'. 'I do not know.' 'It is not the answer.' Người cha cũng dạy cách hỏi lịch sự: bắt đầu bằng 'Madam' / 'Sir' và dùng câu hỏi 'yes/no' với từ 'is/are' ở đầu: 'Is this the oldest tree?' Nói chậm và rõ là một phần của lịch sự.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'do not / does not + base verb' to make a negative. 'I do not know.' 'It is not the answer.' For polite yes/no questions, start with 'is/are': 'Is this the oldest tree?' Speaking slowly and using a title like 'Madam' or 'Sir' is part of being polite.

translationVi:
Lin: Bố ơi, cây này to thật.
Bố: Đúng, Lin. Nó rất to.
Lin: Đây có phải cây già nhất trong vườn không bố?
Bố: Bố không biết.
Lin: Bố lúc nào cũng biết mà. Bố là thầy giáo.
Bố: Bố là thầy giáo toán. Không phải thầy giáo cây.
Lin: Bảng ghi một trăm tám mươi năm.
Bố: Ừ. Vậy là già. Nhưng đó chưa phải câu trả lời.
Lin: Sao bố không đoán?
Bố: Vì đoán không phải là sự thật.
Lin: Nhưng ở nhà bố cũng đoán mà. Bố đoán thời tiết. Bố đoán bữa tối.
Bố: Đúng vậy. Nhưng đây là vườn. Vườn có người biết.
Lin: Ở đâu hả bố?
Bố: Nhìn kìa. Cô đội mũ xanh. Cô làm việc ở đây. Cô biết.
Lin: Bố hỏi cô đi.
Bố: Mình sẽ hỏi. Nhưng Lin nghe bố trước. Nói 'bố không biết' là tốt. Nếu bố nói 'chắc một trăm tám mươi', con sẽ tin bố. Con sẽ kể với bạn. Bạn con sẽ kể với bạn của họ. Một con số sai sẽ lan đi khắp nơi.
Lin: Con hiểu rồi.
Bố: Giờ mình đi hỏi. Câu hỏi là gì?
Lin: 'Cô ơi, đây có phải cây già nhất trong vườn không ạ?'
Bố: Tốt. Nói chậm. Lịch sự.
Lin: Cô ơi, đây có phải cây già nhất không ạ?
Cô: Không. Cây già nhất ở cạnh hồ. Hai trăm mười hai năm tuổi.
Lin: Bố ơi, bố không đoán là đúng.
Bố: Hôm nay con học được hai điều — về cây, và về cách hỏi.

questions.prompt: Where are Lin and her father?
questions.explanation: The title and content describe the gardens — they talk about old trees and a lake.
questions.hint: Look at the summary and the talk about trees and a lake.

questions.explanation: 'I am a teacher of mathematics. Not of trees.'
questions.hint: The father's reply to 'You are a teacher.'

questions.prompt: Why does the father not give an answer?
options: Because he does not know — a guess is not a fact.
questions.explanation: 'Because a guess is not a fact.'
questions.hint: The father's reply to 'Why don't you guess?'

questions.prompt: What does Lin point out about her father?
options: He guesses at home — for the weather and for dinner.
He always gives the right answer.
He has been to the garden many times.
questions.explanation: 'But you guess at home. You guess for the weather. You guess for dinner.'

questions.prompt: What is the father's reason for not guessing today?
options: He is in a bad mood.
A garden has people who know — they can ask them.
Lin will not believe a guess.
questions.hint: Look at what makes 'this' different from home.

questions.prompt: Why does the father say it is good to say 'I do not know'?
options: Because a wrong guess can spread to friends and become a wrong number everywhere.
questions.explanation: 'You will tell your friends. Your friends will tell their friends. Soon a wrong number is everywhere.'
questions.hint: Look at the father's longer speech before they ask the woman.

questions.prompt: How does the father tell Lin to ask the woman?
options: Loud voice, fast — 'How old is this tree?'
Yes, this is the oldest tree.
No — the oldest tree is by the lake, and it is two hundred and twelve years old.
questions.explanation: 'No. The oldest tree is by the lake. Two hundred and twelve years old.'

questions.prompt: What two things does Lin learn today, according to her father?
options: About trees and about how to ask questions
questions.explanation: 'Today you learn two things — about trees, and about questions.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Saying 'I do not know' is sometimes the first step toward a real answer.
Mathematics teachers should not visit gardens.
questions.explanation: The father shows that admitting ignorance leads to asking the right person — and the truth.
questions.hint: Think about why the father refuses to guess.

blanks.explanation: She asks about the oldest tree.
A guess is not a fact.
A garden has people who know.
The woman in the green hat.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The father refuses to guess because a wrong number can spread. Have you ever passed on a 'fact' you weren't sure about, and later found it was wrong? What would change if 'I do not know' were as normal an answer as a guess?
